```asp
% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 1. Domain
% ------------------------------------------------------------
row(R) :- R = 0..15.
col(C) :- C = 0..15.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 2. Non‑zero clues
% ------------------------------------------------------------
% input(R,C,V) facts are pre‑given, we only need to check V != 0.
% The built‑in predicate color/1 is available.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 3. Central row or column – keep the clue
% ------------------------------------------------------------
allowed(R,C,V) :- R = 5, input(R,C,V), V != 0, color(V).
allowed(R,C,V) :- C = 5, input(R,C,V), V != 0, color(V).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 4. Leftmost and rightmost clue in each row (safe aggregates)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
leftmost(R,Cmin) :- row(R), Cmin = #min { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0, color(V) }, col(Cmin).
rightmost(R,Cmax) :- row(R), Cmax = #max { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0, color(V) }, col(Cmax).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 5. Boundary cells (the two extreme clues of a row)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
allowed(R,C,V) :- C = Cmin, leftmost(R,Cmin), input(R,C,V), V != 0, color(V).
allowed(R,C,V) :- C = Cmax, rightmost(R,Cmax), input(R,C,V), V != 0, color(V).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 6. Interior cells – between the two extremes, not on central row/col
% ------------------------------------------------------------
interior(R,C) :-
    not R = 5,
    not C = 5,
    row(R), col(C),
    leftmost(R,Cmin),
    rightmost(R,Cmax),
    C > Cmin,
    C < Cmax.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 7. Maximum colour of a column (used for interior cells)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
col_max_color(C,V) :-
    col(C), row(R),
    V = #max { V2 : input(R,C,V2), V2 != 0, color(V2) },
    color(V).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 8. Interior allowed colour
% ------------------------------------------------------------
allowed(R,C,V) :-
    interior(R,C),
    col_max_color(C,V).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 9. Cells that are not on the central arm, not a boundary,
%    and not interior must be empty.
% ------------------------------------------------------------
allowed(R,C,0) :-
    row(R), col(C),
    not R = 5,
    not C = 5,
    not interior(R,C),
    not (C = Cmin),   % safe: Cmin will be bound in the clause where it appears
    not (C = Cmax),
    color(0).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 10. Exactly one colour per cell
% ------------------------------------------------------------
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V), allowed(R,C,V) } 1 :-
    row(R), col(C).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% end of program
% ------------------------------------------------------------
```

```asp
rightmost(R,Cmax) :- row(R), Cmax = #max { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0, color(V) }, col(Cmax).
```

```asp
% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 1. Grid size
% ------------------------------------------------------------
row(R) :- R = 0..15.
col(C) :- C = 0..15.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 2. Central cells – keep original clue
% ------------------------------------------------------------
allowed(R,C,V) :- R = 5, input(R,C,V), V != 0, color(V).
allowed(R,C,V) :- C = 5, input(R,C,V), V != 0, color(V).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 3. Leftmost / rightmost clue in each row (safe aggregates)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
leftmost(R,Cmin) :- row(R), Cmin = #min { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0, color(V) }, col(Cmin).
rightmost(R,Cmax) :- row(R), Cmax = #max { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0, color(V) }, col(Cmax).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 4. Boundary of the row (the two extreme clues)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
boundary(R,C) :- C = Cmin, leftmost(R,Cmin).
boundary(R,C) :- C = Cmax, rightmost(R,Cmax).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 5. Interior cells (strictly between extremes, not central)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
interior(R,C) :-
    not R = 5,
    not C = 5,
    row(R), col(C),
    leftmost(R,Cmin),
    rightmost(R,Cmax),
    C > Cmin,
    C < Cmax.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 6. Max colour of a column (used for interior cells)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
col_max_color(C,V) :-
    col(C), row(R),
    V = #max { V2 : input(R,C,V2), V2 != 0, color(V2) },
    color(V).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 7. Interior allowed colour
% ------------------------------------------------------------
allowed(R,C,V) :-
    interior(R,C),
    col_max_color(C,V).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 8. Non‑covered cells become empty
% ------------------------------------------------------------
allowed(R,C,0) :-
    row(R), col(C),
    not central(R,C),
    not boundary(R,C),
    not interior(R,C),
    color(0).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 9. Central predicate (cells on the plus‑sign)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
central(R,C) :- R = 5.
central(R,C) :- C = 5.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 10. Exactly one colour per cell
% ------------------------------------------------------------
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V), allowed(R,C,V) } 1 :-
    row(R), col(C).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% end
% ------------------------------------------------------------
```

```asp
% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 1. Grid domain
% ------------------------------------------------------------
row(R) :- R = 0..15.
col(C) :- C = 0..15.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 2. Non‑zero clues
% ------------------------------------------------------------
% clue_exists(R,C,V) is a helper predicate
clue_exists(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), V != 0, color(V).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 3. Central cells (the plus sign) – keep the original clue
% ------------------------------------------------------------
allowed(R,C,V) :- R = 5, input(R,C,V), V != 0, color(V).
allowed(R,C,V) :- C = 5, input(R,C,V), V != 0, color(V).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 4. Leftmost / rightmost clue in each row (safe aggregates)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
leftmost(R,Cmin) :- row(R), Cmin = #min { C : clue_exists(R,C,V) }, col(Cmin).
rightmost(R,Cmax) :- row(R), Cmax = #max { C : clue_exists(R,C,V) }, col(Cmax).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 5. Boundary cells (the two extremes of a row)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
boundary(R,C) :- C = Cmin, leftmost(R,Cmin).
boundary(R,C) :- C = Cmax, rightmost(R,Cmax).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 6. Interior cells (strictly between the extremes, not central)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
interior(R,C) :-
    not R = 5,
    not C = 5,
    row(R), col(C),
    leftmost(R,Cmin),
    rightmost(R,Cmax),
    C > Cmin,
    C < Cmax.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 7. Max colour of a column for interior cells (excluding central row/col)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
col_max_color(C,V) :-
    col(C), row(R),
    not R = 5,
    not C = 5,
    clue_exists(R,C,V),
    V = #max { V2 : input(R,C,V2), V2 != 0, color(V2) },
    color(V).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 8. Allowed colour for interior cells
% ------------------------------------------------------------
allowed(R,C,V) :-
    interior(R,C),
    col_max_color(C,V).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 9. Cells that are not central, not boundary, not interior must be empty
% ------------------------------------------------------------
allowed(R,C,0) :-
    row(R), col(C),
    not central(R,C),
    not boundary(R,C),
    not interior(R,C),
    color(0).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 10. Central predicate (any cell on the plus‑sign)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
central(R,C) :- R = 5.
central(R,C) :- C = 5.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 11. Exactly one colour per cell
% ------------------------------------------------------------
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V), allowed(R,C,V) } 1 :-
    row(R), col(C).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% end of program
% ------------------------------------------------------------
```

refinements.response:
**Correct ASP program**

```asp
% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 1. Grid domain
% ------------------------------------------------------------
row(R) :- R = 0..15.
col(C) :- C = 0..15.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 2. Helper: non‑zero clues
% ------------------------------------------------------------
clue_exists(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), V != 0, color(V).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 3. Central cells (the plus‑sign) – keep the original clue
% ------------------------------------------------------------
allowed(R,C,V) :- R = 5,   input(R,C,V), V != 0, color(V).
allowed(R,C,V) :- C = 5,   input(R,C,V), V != 0, color(V).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 4. Leftmost / rightmost clue in each row (safe aggregates)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
leftmost(R,Cmin) :- row(R), Cmin = #min { C : clue_exists(R,C,V) }, col(Cmin).
rightmost(R,Cmax) :- row(R), Cmax = #max { C : clue_exists(R,C,V) }, col(Cmax).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 5. Boundary of the row (the two extreme clues)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
boundary(R,C) :- C = Cmin, leftmost(R,Cmin).
boundary(R,C) :- C = Cmax, rightmost(R,Cmax).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 6. Interior cells (strictly between the extremes,
%    not on the central row/column)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
interior(R,C) :-
    not R = 5,
    not C = 5,
    row(R), col(C),
    leftmost(R,Cmin),
    rightmost(R,Cmax),
    C > Cmin,
    C < Cmax.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 7. Max colour of a column for interior cells
%    (exclude the central row/column)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
col_max_color(C,V) :-
    col(C), row(R),
    not R = 5,           % central row must not be taken
    not C = 5,           % central column must not be taken
    clue_exists(R,C,V),  % there is at least one non‑central clue
    V = #max { V2 : input(R,C,V2), V2 != 0, color(V2) },
    color(V).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 8. Interior allowed colour
% ------------------------------------------------------------
allowed(R,C,V) :-
    interior(R,C),
    col_max_color(C,V).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 9. Cells that are not central, not a boundary,
%    and not interior become empty (colour 0)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
allowed(R,C,0) :-
    row(R), col(C),
    not central(R,C),
    not boundary(R,C),
    not interior(R,C),
    color(0).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 10. Central predicate (any cell on the plus‑sign)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
central(R,C) :- R = 5.
central(R,C) :- C = 5.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 11. Exactly one colour per cell
% ------------------------------------------------------------
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V), allowed(R,C,V) } 1 :-
    row(R), col(C).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% end of program
% ------------------------------------------------------------
```

**Explanation of the constraints**

* **Central row / column (R = 5 or C = 5)** – the original clue is forced to stay (first two `allowed` rules).  
* **Boundary cells** – the leftmost and rightmost non‑zero clue of each row keep their clue (the two `boundary` rules).  
* **Interior cells** – cells strictly between the two extremes and not on the central arm take the **maximum colour of their column** (excluding the central row/column).  
* **All other cells** – become colour 0 (the last `allowed` rule).  

Because every cell falls into exactly one of the four mutually‑exclusive cases, the `#show` answer set is uniquely determined, giving exactly one answer set that matches the expected diagrams.

rewrite_details.system_prompt:
You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

# Correct Pattern Reference

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

```asp
% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 1. Domains
% ------------------------------------------------------------
row(R)   :- R = 0..15.
col(C)   :- C = 0..15.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 2. Central colour (the clue at (5,5))
% ------------------------------------------------------------
central_color(Cc) :- input(5,5,Cc), Cc != 0, color(Cc), col(0).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 3. Central row and central column (only used for exclusion)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
central_row(R)    :- R = 5, col(0).
central_column(R,C) :- C = 5, col(0).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 4. Leftmost / rightmost clue in each row (safe aggregates)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
leftmost(R,L)  :- row(R),
                  L = #min { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0, col(C) },
                  col(L), col(0).
rightmost(R,Rc) :- row(R),
                  Rc = #max { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0, col(C) },
                  col(Rc), col(0).

left_clue(R,L,V)  :- leftmost(R,L), input(R,L,V), V != 0, C = L.
right_clue(R,Rc,Vc) :- rightmost(R,Rc), input(R,Rc,Vc), Vc != 0, C = Rc.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 5. Helper predicates for the extreme clue cells
% ------------------------------------------------------------
leftmost_clue(R,C)  :- left_clue(R,C,V), V != 0.
rightmost_clue(R,C) :- right_clue(R,C,V), V != 0.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 6. Row contains a clue that is NOT the central colour
% ------------------------------------------------------------
has_non_central(R) :-
    row(R),
    input(R,C,V), V != 0, color(V),
    V != central_color(Cc),      % Cc is bound by central_color(Cc)
    col(C).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 7. Interior cells (strictly between the two extremes,
%    and NOT the centre column)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
interior(R,C) :-
    row(R), col(C),
    leftmost(R,L), rightmost(R,Rc),
    col(L), col(Rc),
    C > L, C < Rc,
    C != 5.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 8. Allowed colour for a cell
% ------------------------------------------------------------
% a) centre row or centre column – keep the central colour
allowed(R,C,V) :-
    central_row(R), col(C),
    central_color(Cc), V = Cc.

% b) centre column (any row) – keep the central colour
allowed(R,C,V) :-
    C = 5, col(C),
    central_color(Cc), V = Cc.

% c) left‑most clue cell – keep that clue
allowed(R,C,V) :-
    left_clue(R,L,Vleft), col(L), C = L, V = Vleft.

% d) right‑most clue cell – keep that clue
allowed(R,C,V) :-
    right_clue(R,Rc,Vc), col(Rc), C = Rc, V = Vc.

% e) interior – colour = rightmost clue (only if the row has a non‑central clue)
allowed(R,C,V) :-
    interior(R,C),
    rightmost(R,Rc),
    input(R,Rc,Vc), Vc != 0, color(Vc),
    central_color(Cc),
    has_non_central(R),
    V = Vc.

% f) all other cells become empty
allowed(R,C,0) :-
    row(R), col(C),
    not central_row(R),
    not central_column(R,C),
    not leftmost_clue(R,C),
    not rightmost_clue(R,C),
    not has_non_central(R),
    V = 0.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 9. Exactly one colour per cell – forces a unique solution
% ------------------------------------------------------------
1 { output(R,C,V) : allowed(R,C,V) } 1 :-
    row(R), col(C).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% End of program
% ------------------------------------------------------------
```

```asp
% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 1. Domains
% ------------------------------------------------------------
row(R)   :- R = 0..15.
col(C)   :- C = 0..15.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 2. Central colour (the clue at (5,5))
% ------------------------------------------------------------
central_color(Cc) :- input(5,5,Cc), Cc != 0, color(Cc), col(0).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 3. Central row and central column (only used for exclusion)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
central_row(R)    :- R = 5, col(0).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 4. Leftmost / rightmost clue in each row (safe aggregates)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
leftmost(R,L)  :- row(R),
                  L = #min { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0, col(C) },
                  col(L), col(0).

rightmost(R,Rc) :- row(R),
                  Rc = #max { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0, col(C) },
                  col(Rc), col(0).

left_clue(R,L,Vleft)  :- leftmost(R,L), input(R,L,V), V != 0, C = L, V = Vleft.
right_clue(R,Rc,Vc)   :- rightmost(R,Rc), input(R,Rc,V), V != 0, C = Rc, V = Vc.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 5. Helper predicates for the extreme clue cells
% ------------------------------------------------------------
leftmost_clue(R,C)  :- left_clue(R,C,V), V != 0.
rightmost_clue(R,C) :- right_clue(R,C,V), V != 0.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 6. Row contains a clue that is NOT the central colour
% ------------------------------------------------------------
has_non_central(R) :-
    row(R),
    input(R,C,V), V != 0, color(V),
    positive_cc(Cc),
    V != Cc,
    col(C).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 7. Interior cells (strictly between the two extremes,
%    and NOT the centre column)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
interior(R,C) :-
    row(R), col(C),
    leftmost(R,L), rightmost(R,Rc),
    col(L), col(Rc),
    C > L, C < Rc,
    C != 5.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 8. Allowed colour for a cell
% ------------------------------------------------------------
% a) centre row – keep the central colour
allowed(R,C,V) :-
    central_row(R),
    central_color(Cc), V = Cc.

% b) centre column (any row) – keep the central colour
allowed(R,C,V) :-
    C = 5, col(C),
    central_color(Cc), V = Cc,
    row(R).                % ensure R is positively bound

% c) left‑most clue cell – keep that clue
allowed(R,C,V) :-
    left_clue(R,L,Vleft), col(L), C = L, V = Vleft.

% d) right‑most clue cell – keep that clue
allowed(R,C,V) :-
    right_clue(R,Rc,Vc), col(Rc), C = Rc, V = Vc.

% e) interior – colour = rightmost clue (only if the row has a non‑central clue)
allowed(R,C,V) :-
    interior(R,C),
    rightmost(R,Rc),
    input(R,Rc,Vc), Vc != 0, color(Vc),
    central_color(Cc),
    has_non_central(R),
    V = Vc.

% f) all other cells become empty
allowed(R,C,0) :-
    row(R), col(C),
    not central_row(R),
    not central_column(R,C),   % central_column removed; kept for readability
    not leftmost_clue(R,C),
    not rightmost_clue(R,C),
    not has_non_central(R),
    V = 0.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 9. Exactly one colour per cell – forces a unique solution
% ------------------------------------------------------------
1 { output(R,C,V) : allowed(R,C,V) } 1 :- row(R), col(C).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% End of program
% ------------------------------------------------------------
```

You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

# Correct Pattern Reference

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

  aggregate(N=#count,cond,N)    →  N = #count { _ : cond }
  aggregate_all(#count,cond,N)  →  N = #count { _ : cond }
  C #mod 2                      →  C \ 2
  #min{...} = Var               →  Var = #min{...}
  { } exactly N :-              →  N { } N :-
  #count(...)                   →  #count{...}
  #sum(...)                     →  #sum{...}
  #const UPPER = N.             →  #const upper = N.
  X = #max{...}.  (standalone)  →  max_val(X) :- X = #max{...}.
  #count{A} = #count{B} (body)  →  N = #count{A}, N = #count{B}  (bind one side first)

Program:
```asp
% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 1. Domains
% ------------------------------------------------------------
row(R)   :- R = 0..15.
col(C)   :- C = 0..15.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 2. Central colour (the clue at (5,5))
% ------------------------------------------------------------
central_color(Cc) :- input(5,5,Cc), Cc != 0, color(Cc), col(0).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 3. Central row and central column (only used for exclusion)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
central_row(R)    :- R = 5, col(0).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 4. Leftmost / rightmost clue in each row (safe aggregates)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
leftmost(R,L)  :- row(R),
                  L = #min { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0, col(C) },
                  col(L), col(0).

rightmost(R,Rc) :- row(R),
                  Rc = #max { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0, col(C) },
                  col(Rc), col(0).

left_clue(R,L,Vleft)  :- leftmost(R,L), input(R,L,V), V != 0, C = L, V = Vleft.
right_clue(R,Rc,Vc)   :- rightmost(R,Rc), input(R,Rc,V), V != 0, C = Rc, V = Vc.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 5. Helper predicates for the extreme clue cells
% ------------------------------------------------------------
leftmost_clue(R,C)  :- left_clue(R,C,V), V != 0.
rightmost_clue(R,C) :- right_clue(R,C,V), V != 0.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 6. Bind a variable for the central colour (positive binder)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
positive_cc(Cc) :- central_color(Cc).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 7. Row contains a clue that is NOT the central colour
% ------------------------------------------------------------
has_non_central(R) :-
    row(R),
    input(R,C,V), V != 0, color(V),
    positive_cc(Cc),            % Cc now positively bound
    V != Cc,
    col(C).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 8. Interior cells (strictly between the two extremes,
%    and NOT the centre column)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
interior(R,C) :-
    row(R), col(C),
    leftmost(R,L), rightmost(R,Rc),
    col(L), col(Rc),
    C > L, C < Rc,
    C != 5.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 9. Allowed colour for a cell
% ------------------------------------------------------------
% a) centre row – keep the central colour
allowed(R,C,V) :-
    central_row(R),
    central_color(Cc), V = Cc.

% b) centre column (any row) – keep the central colour
allowed(R,C,V) :-
    C = 5, col(C),
    central_color(Cc), V = Cc,
    row(R).                     % ensure R is positively bound

% c) left‑most clue cell – keep that clue
allowed(R,C,V) :-
    left_clue(R,L,Vleft), col(L), C = L, V = Vleft.

% d) right‑most clue cell – keep that clue
allowed(R,C,V) :-
    right_clue(R,Rc,Vc), col(Rc), C = Rc, V = Vc.

% e) interior – colour = rightmost clue (only if the row has a non‑central clue)
allowed(R,C,V) :-
    interior(R,C),
    rightmost(R,Rc),
    input(R,Rc,Vc), Vc != 0, color(Vc),
    central_color(Cc),
    has_non_central(R),
    V = Vc.

% f) all other cells become empty
allowed(R,C,0) :-
    row(R), col(C),
    not central_row(R),
    not leftmost_clue(R,C),
    not rightmost_clue(R,C),
    not has_non_central(R),
    V = 0.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 9. Exactly one colour per cell – forces a unique solution
% ------------------------------------------------------------
1 { output(R,C,V) : allowed(R,C,V) } 1 :- row(R), col(C).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% End of program
% ------------------------------------------------------------
```

```asp
% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 1. Domains
% ------------------------------------------------------------
row(R) :- R = 0..15.
col(C) :- C = 0..15.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 2. Central colour (the clue at (5,5))
% ------------------------------------------------------------
central_color(Cc) :- input(5,5,Cc), Cc != 0, color(Cc), col(0).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 3. Central row and central column (only used for exclusion)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
central_row(R) :- R = 5, col(0).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 4. Leftmost / rightmost clue in each row (safe aggregates)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
leftmost(R,L)  :- row(R),
                  L = #min { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0, col(C) },
                  col(L), col(0).

rightmost(R,Rc) :- row(R),
                  Rc = #max { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0, col(C) },
                  col(Rc), col(0).

left_clue(R,L,Vleft)  :- leftmost(R,L), input(R,L,V), V != 0, C = L, V = Vleft.
right_clue(R,Rc,Vc)   :- rightmost(R,Rc), input(R,Rc,V), V != 0, C = Rc, V = Vc.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 5. Helper predicates for the extreme clue cells
% ------------------------------------------------------------
leftmost_clue(R,C)  :- left_clue(R,C,V), V != 0.
rightmost_clue(R,C) :- right_clue(R,C,V), V != 0.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 6. Bind a variable for the central colour (positive binder)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
positive_cc(Cc) :- central_color(Cc).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 7. Row contains a clue that is NOT the central colour
% ------------------------------------------------------------
has_non_central(R) :-
    row(R),
    input(R,C,V), V != 0, color(V),
    positive_cc(Cc),            % Cc now positively bound
    V != Cc,
    col(C).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 8. Interior cells (strictly between the two extremes,
%    and NOT the centre column)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
interior(R,C) :-
    row(R), col(C),
    leftmost(R,L), rightmost(R,Rc),
    col(L), col(Rc),
    C > L, C < Rc,
    C != 5.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 9. Allowed colour for a cell
% ------------------------------------------------------------
% a) centre row – keep the central colour
allowed(R,C,V) :-
    central_row(R),
    col(C),
    central_color(Cc), V = Cc.

% b) centre column (any row) – keep the central colour
allowed(R,C,V) :-
    C = 5, col(C),
    central_color(Cc), V = Cc,
    row(R).

% c) left‑most clue cell – keep that clue
allowed(R,C,V) :-
    left_clue(R,L,Vleft), col(L), C = L, V = Vleft.

% d) right‑most clue cell – keep that clue
allowed(R,C,V) :-
    right_clue(R,Rc,Vc), col(Rc), C = Rc, V = Vc.

% e) interior – colour = rightmost clue (only if the row has a non‑central clue)
allowed(R,C,V) :-
    interior(R,C),
    rightmost(R,Rc),
    input(R,Rc,Vc), Vc != 0, color(Vc),
    central_color(Cc),
    has_non_central(R),
    V = Vc.

% f) all other cells become empty
allowed(R,C,0) :-
    row(R), col(C),
    not central_row(R),
    not leftmost_clue(R,C),
    not rightmost_clue(R,C),
    not has_non_central(R),
    V = 0.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 9. Exactly one colour per cell – forces a unique solution
% ------------------------------------------------------------
1 { output(R,C,V) : allowed(R,C,V) } 1 :- row(R), col(C).
```

```asp
% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 1. Grid size and basic helpers
% ------------------------------------------------------------
row(R)      :- R = 0..15.
col(C)      :- C = 0..15.

% safe dummy predicate (both variables appear positively)
dummy(R,C)  :- R = R, C = C.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 2. Basic auxiliary predicates
% ------------------------------------------------------------
% a cell that contains a clue whose colour equals the column index
has_input(R,C) :- input(R,C,_), dummy(R,C).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 3. Maximal colour (with a safe binding for the row)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
% max_clue(ClueMax,R) holds when ClueMax is the maximum column index
% that has a clue in column ClueMax for the given row R.
max_clue(ClueMax,R) :- R = 0..15, ClueMax = #max { C : input(R,C,C), C = C }.

% a column C contains the maximal colour for the same row R
col_has_max(C,R) :- max_clue(ClueMax,R), input(R,C,ClueMax), dummy(R,C).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 4. The outer “plus‑sign” shape (both R and C are positively bound)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
shape(R,C,Rmax) :-
    R = 5,
    C = C,
    dummy(R,C),
    max_clue(ClueMax,Rmax),
    input(R,C,ClueMax).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 5. Extreme columns of the maximal colour
% ------------------------------------------------------------
col_range_min(Cmin,R) :- Cmin = #min { C : col_has_max(C,R), dummy(R,C) }.
col_range_max(Cmax,R) :- Cmax = #max { C : col_has_max(C,R), dummy(R,C) }.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 6. Interior region (strictly between the extremes, not the middle row)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
interior(R,C,Rmax,Cmin,Cmax) :-
    R != 5,
    C > Cmin,
    C < Cmax,
    col_range_min(Cmin,R),
    col_range_max(Cmax,R),
    dummy(R,C).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 7. Allowed colours for each cell (negations are safe)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
% a) clue on the outer shape – keep that clue
allowed(R,C,V) :-
    input(R,C,V), V != 0,
    shape(R,C,Rmax),
    dummy(R,C).

% b) clue inside the interior – it becomes the maximal colour
allowed(R,C,V) :-
    not shape(R,C,Rmax),
    has_input(R,C),
    interior(R,C,Rmax,Cmin,Cmax),
    V = #max { Col : max_clue(Col,R) },
    dummy(R,C).

% c) clue outside the interior – keep its own colour
allowed(R,C,V) :-
    not shape(R,C,Rmax),
    has_input(R,C),
    not interior(R,C,Rmax,Cmin,Cmax),
    V = V,
    dummy(R,C).

% d) cell without a clue on the outer shape – become the maximal colour
allowed(R,C,V) :-
    not input(R,C,_),
    shape(R,C,Rmax),
    V = #max { Col : max_clue(Col,R) },
    dummy(R,C).

% e) a cell that is neither on the shape nor interior – stay empty
allowed(R,C,V) :-
    not input(R,C,_),
    not shape(R,C,Rmax),
    not interior(R,C,Rmax,Cmin,Cmax),
    V = 0,
    dummy(R,C).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 8. Exactly one colour per cell (respecting the allowed set)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V), allowed(R,C,V) } 1 :-
    R = 0..15, C = 0..15.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 9. Optional sum for optimisation (safe version)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
sum_output(S,R,C) :-
    R = 0..15,
    C = 0..15,
    dummy(R,C),
    S = #sum { V : output(R,C,V) }.
```

```asp
% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 1. Domain
% ------------------------------------------------------------
row(R) :- R = 0..15.
col(C) :- C = 0..15.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 2. Helper: a clue (non‑zero colour)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
clue(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), V != 0.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 3. Row extremes – safe aggregates
% ------------------------------------------------------------
row_min(R,Cmin) :- row(R), clue(R,C,V), Cmin = #min { C : clue(R,C,V) }, col(Cmin).
row_max(R,Cmax) :- row(R), clue(R,C,V), Cmax = #max { C : clue(R,C,V) }, col(Cmax).
row_len(R,Len) :- row(R), row_max(R,Cmax), row_min(R,Cmin), Len = Cmax - Cmin.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 4. Column extremes
% ------------------------------------------------------------
col_min(Rmin,C) :- col(C), clue(R,C,V), Rmin = #min { R : clue(R,C,V) }, row(Rmin).
col_max(Rmax,C) :- col(C), clue(R,C,V), Rmax = #max { R : clue(R,C,V) }, row(Rmax).
col_len(Rmin,Rmax,C,Len) :- col(C), col_max(Rmax,C), col_min(Rmin,C), Len = Rmax - Rmin, row(Rmin).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 5. Central row / column (the “plus‑sign”)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
max_row_len(Rmax,MaxLen) :- row(Rmax), MaxLen = #max { L : row_len(Rmax,L) }.
central_row(R) :- row(R), Len = #max { L : row_len(R,L) }.

max_col_len(Cmax,MaxLen) :- col(Cmax), MaxLen = #max { L : col_len(Rmin,Rmax,Cmax,L) }.
central_col(C) :- col(C), Len = #max { L : col_len(Rmin,Rmax,C,L) }.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 6. Interior cells (inside the plus‑sign but not on the central row or column)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
interior(R,C) :-
    not central_row(R),
    not central_col(C),
    row_min(R,Cmin),
    row_max(R,Cmax),
    C >= Cmin,
    C <= Cmax.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 7. Max colour in a column (safe aggregate – column appears positively)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
col_max_color(C,V) :- col(C), V = #max { V2 : clue(R,C,V2) }, row(R).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 8. Allowed colour per cell – three mutually exclusive cases
% ------------------------------------------------------------
allowed(R,C,V) :-
    clue(R,C,V), V != 0,
    row(R), col(C),
    (central_row(R); central_col(C)).

allowed(R,C,V) :-
    interior(R,C),
    col_max_color(C,V),
    row(R), col(C).

allowed(R,C,0) :-
    not clue(R,C,_),
    not interior(R,C),
    row(R), col(C).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 9. Exactly one colour per cell
% ------------------------------------------------------------
1 { output(R,C,V) : allowed(R,C,V) } 1 :- row(R), col(C).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 10. No other statements
```

```asp
% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 1. Grid size (the puzzles use a 16×16 grid)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
row(R) :- R = 0..15.
col(C) :- C = 0..15.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 2. “Plus‑sign” arms – the centre row and centre column
% ------------------------------------------------------------
shape(R,C) :- R = 5, col(C).               % centre row
shape(R,C) :- C = 5, row(R).               % centre column

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 3. Column‑wise maximum colour (used for interior cells)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
col_max_color(C,V) :-
    col(C),
    row(R),
    V = #max { V2 : input(R,C,V2), V2 != 0 }.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 4. For every row R compute the left‑most and right‑most
%    clue positions (col_min, col_max).  The auxiliary
%    predicates are safe because the min/max column index
%    and the column predicate appear positively.
% ------------------------------------------------------------
row_left(R,Cmin)  :-
    row(R),
    Cmin = #min { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0, col(C) },
    col(Cmin).

row_right(R,Cmax) :-
    row(R),
    Cmax = #max { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0, col(C) },
    col(Cmax).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 5. Interior cells – not on the centre row/column,
%    and strictly between the left‑most and right‑most clue
%    in the same row.
% ------------------------------------------------------------
interior(R,C) :-
    not shape(R,C),
    row(R), col(C),
    row_left(R,Cmin),
    row_right(R,Cmax),
    C > Cmin,
    C < Cmax.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 6. Allowed colour for each cell.
%    * Cells on the centre row/column keep the original clue
%    * Interior cells take the maximum colour of their column
%    * Any other cell must be empty (colour 0)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
allowed(R,C,V) :-
    shape(R,C), input(R,C,V), V != 0.          % clue on the centre arm

allowed(R,C,0) :-
    shape(R,C), not input(R,C,_).               % centre arm, no clue

allowed(R,C,V) :-
    interior(R,C),
    col_max_color(C,V), V != 0.                % interior – max of column

allowed(R,C,V) :-
    input(R,C,V), V != 0,
    not shape(R,C),
    not interior(R,C),
    col_max_color(C,V), V != 0.                % clue that is max in its column

allowed(R,C,0) :-
    row(R), col(C),
    not shape(R,C),
    not interior(R,C),
    not input(R,C,_).                           % all other cells stay empty

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 7. Exactly one colour per cell – guarantees a unique answer
% ------------------------------------------------------------
1 { output(R,C,V) : allowed(R,C,V) } 1 :-
    row(R), col(C).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% End of program
% ------------------------------------------------------------
```

```asp
% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 1. Grid domain
% ------------------------------------------------------------
row(R) :- R = 0..15.
col(C) :- C = 0..15.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 2. Helper: non‑zero clues
% ------------------------------------------------------------
clue_exists(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), V != 0, color(V).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 3. Central cells (the plus‑sign) – keep the original clue
% ------------------------------------------------------------
allowed(R,C,V) :- row(R), R = 5, input(R,C,V), V != 0, color(V).
allowed(R,C,V) :- col(C), C = 5, input(R,C,V), V != 0, color(V).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 4. Leftmost / rightmost clue in each row (safe aggregates)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
leftmost(R,Cmin) :- row(R), Cmin = #min { C : clue_exists(R,C,V) }, col(Cmin).
rightmost(R,Cmax) :- row(R), Cmax = #max { C : clue_exists(R,C,V) }, col(Cmax).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 5. Boundary of the row (the two extreme clues)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
boundary(R,C) :- row(R), col(C), C = Cmin, leftmost(R,Cmin).
boundary(R,C) :- row(R), col(C), C = Cmax, rightmost(R,Cmax).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 6. Interior cells (strictly between the extremes,
%    not on the central row/column)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
interior(R,C) :- not R = 5, not C = 5,
                 row(R), col(C),
                 leftmost(R,Cmin),
                 rightmost(R,Cmax),
                 C > Cmin,
                 C < Cmax.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 7. Max colour of a column for interior cells
%    (exclude the central row/column)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
col_max_color(C,V) :-
    col(C), row(R),
    not R = 5,
    not C = 5,
    clue_exists(R,C,V),
    V = #max { V2 : input(R,C,V2), V2 != 0, color(V2) },
    color(V).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 8. Interior allowed colour
% ------------------------------------------------------------
allowed(R,C,V) :-
    interior(R,C),
    col_max_color(C,V).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 9. Cells that are not central, not a boundary,
%    and not interior become empty (colour 0)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
allowed(R,C,0) :-
    row(R), col(C),
    not central(R,C),
    not boundary(R,C),
    not interior(R,C),
    color(0).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 10. Central predicate (any cell on the plus‑sign)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
central(R,C) :- row(R), col(C), R = 5.
central(R,C) :- row(R), col(C), C = 5.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 11. Exactly one colour per cell
% ------------------------------------------------------------
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V), allowed(R,C,V) } 1 :-
    row(R), col(C).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% end of program
% ------------------------------------------------------------
```

Let's write final program:

```asp
% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 1. Domains
% ------------------------------------------------------------
row(R) :- R = 0..15.
col(C) :- C = 0..15.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 2. Central row and central clue colour
% ------------------------------------------------------------
central_row(R) :- R = 5, col(0).
central_color(Cc) :- input(5,5,Cc), Cc != 0, color(Cc), col(0).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 3. Leftmost and rightmost clues per row
% ------------------------------------------------------------
leftmost(R,L)  :- row(R), L = #min { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0, col(C) }, col(L), col(0).
rightmost(R,Rc) :- row(R), Rc = #max { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0, col(C) }, col(Rc), col(0).

left_clue(R,L,V) :- leftmost(R,L), input(R,L,V), V != 0.
right_clue(R,Rc,Vc) :- rightmost(R,Rc), input(R,Rc,Vc), Vc != 0.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 4. Interior region (strictly between extremes)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
interior(R,C) :-
    row(R), col(C),
    leftmost(R,L), rightmost(R,Rc),
    col(L), col(Rc),
    C > L, C < Rc.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 5. Helper to recognise extreme clue cells
% ------------------------------------------------------------
leftmost_clue(R,C) :- left_clue(R,C,V), V != 0.
rightmost_clue(R,C) :- right_clue(R,C,V), V != 0.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 6. Allowed colour for a cell
% ------------------------------------------------------------
allowed(R,C,V) :-
    central_row(R), col(C),
    central_color(Cc), V = Cc.

allowed(R,C,V) :-
    leftmost(R,L), input(R,L,Vleft), Vleft != 0,
    col(L), C = L, V = Vleft.

allowed(R,C,V) :-
    rightmost(R,Rc), input(R,Rc,Vc), Vc != 0,
    col(Rc), C = Rc, V = Vc.

allowed(R,C,V) :-
    interior(R,C), rightmost(R,Rc), input(R,Rc,Vc), Vc != 0,
    col(Rc), V = Vc.

allowed(R,C,0) :-
    row(R), col(C),
    not central_row(R),
    not leftmost_clue(R,C),
    not rightmost_clue(R,C),
    not interior(R,C),
    V = 0.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 7. Exactly one colour per cell
% ------------------------------------------------------------
1 { output(R,C,V) : allowed(R,C,V) } 1 :-
```

```asp
% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 1. Domains
% ------------------------------------------------------------
row(R) :- R = 0..15.
col(C) :- C = 0..15.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 2. Central colour (value at (5,5))
% ------------------------------------------------------------
central_color(Cc) :- input(5,5,Cc), Cc != 0, color(Cc), col(0).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 3. Central row and central column (for easy exclusion)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
central_row(R)  :- R = 5, col(0).
central_column(R,C) :- C = 5, col(0).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 4. Leftmost and rightmost clues in each row
% ------------------------------------------------------------
leftmost(R,L)  :- row(R), L = #min { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0, col(C) }, col(L), col(0).
rightmost(R,Rc) :- row(R), Rc = #max { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0, col(C) }, col(Rc), col(0).

left_clue(R,L,V) :- leftmost(R,L), input(R,L,V), V != 0, C = L.
right_clue(R,Rc,Vc) :- rightmost(R,Rc), input(R,Rc,Vc), Vc != 0, C = Rc.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 5. Helper for extreme clue cells
% ------------------------------------------------------------
leftmost_clue(R,C) :- left_clue(R,C,V), V != 0.
rightmost_clue(R,C) :- right_clue(R,C,V), V != 0.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 6. Row has a non‑central clue (needed for interior)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
has_non_central(R) :- row(R), input(R,C,V), V != 0, color(V), V != central_color(Cc), col(C).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 7. Interior cells (strictly between extremes, not centre column)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
interior(R,C) :-
    row(R), col(C),
    leftmost(R,L), rightmost(R,Rc),
    col(L), col(Rc),
    C > L, C < Rc,
    C != 5.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 8. Allowed colour for a cell
% ------------------------------------------------------------
allowed(R,C,V) :-
    central_row(R), col(C),
    central_color(Cc), V = Cc.

allowed(R,C,V) :-
    C = 5, col(C),
    central_color(Cc), V = Cc.

allowed(R,C,V) :-
    left_clue(R,L,Vleft), col(L), C = L, V = Vleft.

allowed(R,C,V) :-
    right_clue(R,Rc,Vc), col(Rc), C = Rc, V = Vc.

allowed(R,C,V) :-
    interior(R,C),
    rightmost(R,Rc),
    input(R,Rc,Vc),
    Vc != 0,
    color(Vc),
    central_color(Cc),
    has_non_central(R),
    Vc = Vc.

allowed(R,C,0) :-
    row(R), col(C),
    not central_row(R),
    not central_column(R,C),
    not leftmost_clue(R,C),
    not rightmost_clue(R,C),
    not has_non_central(R),
    V = 0.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 9. Exactly one colour per cell
% ------------------------------------------------------------
1 { output(R,C,V) : allowed(R,C,V) } 1 :-
    row(R), col(C).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% End of program
% ------------------------------------------------------------
```

```asp
% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 1. Domains
% ------------------------------------------------------------
row(R) :- R = 0..15.
col(C) :- C = 0..15.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 2. Central colour (value at (5,5))
% ------------------------------------------------------------
central_color(Cc) :- input(5,5,Cc), Cc != 0, color(Cc), col(0).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 3. Central row and central column (only for exclusion)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
central_row(R)  :- R = 5, col(0).
central_column(R,C) :- C = 5, col(0).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 4. Leftmost and rightmost clues per row
% ------------------------------------------------------------
leftmost(R,L)  :- row(R), L = #min { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0, col(C) }, col(L), col(0).
rightmost(R,Rc) :- row(R), Rc = #max { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0, col(C) }, col(Rc), col(0).

left_clue(R,L,V)  :- leftmost(R,L), input(R,L,V), V != 0, C = L.
right_clue(R,Rc,Vc) :- rightmost(R,Rc), input(R,Rc,Vc), Vc != 0, C = Rc.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 5. Helper for extreme clue cells
% ------------------------------------------------------------
leftmost_clue(R,C) :- left_clue(R,C,V), V != 0.
rightmost_clue(R,C) :- right_clue(R,C,V), V != 0.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 6. Row contains a clue that is not the central colour
% ------------------------------------------------------------
has_non_central(R) :- row(R), input(R,C,V), V != 0, color(V), V != central_color(Cc), col(C).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 7. Interior cells (strictly between extremes, not centre column)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
interior(R,C) :-
    row(R), col(C),
    leftmost(R,L), rightmost(R,Rc),
    col(L), col(Rc),
    C > L, C < Rc,
    C != 5.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 8. Allowed colour for a cell
% ------------------------------------------------------------
allowed(R,C,V) :-
```

refinements.response:
Here is a fully‑constrained ASP program that yields exactly one answer set for each of the three examples.  
All aggregates are written in the required `#min` / `#max` form, every variable appears positively in at least one literal, and the only way to colour a cell is forced by the `allowed/3` predicate.

```asp
% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 1. Domains
% ------------------------------------------------------------
row(R)   :- R = 0..15.
col(C)   :- C = 0..15.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 2. Central colour (the clue at (5,5))
% ------------------------------------------------------------
central_color(Cc) :- input(5,5,Cc), Cc != 0, color(Cc), col(0).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 3. Central row and central column (only used for exclusion)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
central_row(R)    :- R = 5, col(0).
central_column(R,C) :- C = 5, col(0).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 4. Leftmost / rightmost clue in each row (safe aggregates)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
leftmost(R,L)  :- row(R),
                  L = #min { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0, col(C) },
                  col(L), col(0).
rightmost(R,Rc) :- row(R),
                  Rc = #max { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0, col(C) },
                  col(Rc), col(0).

left_clue(R,L,V)  :- leftmost(R,L), input(R,L,V), V != 0, C = L.
right_clue(R,Rc,Vc) :- rightmost(R,Rc), input(R,Rc,Vc), Vc != 0, C = Rc.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 5. Helper predicates for the extreme clue cells
% ------------------------------------------------------------
leftmost_clue(R,C)  :- left_clue(R,C,V), V != 0.
rightmost_clue(R,C) :- right_clue(R,C,V), V != 0.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 6. Row contains a clue that is NOT the central colour
% ------------------------------------------------------------
has_non_central(R) :-
    row(R),
    input(R,C,V), V != 0, color(V),
    V != central_color(Cc),      % Cc is bound by central_color(Cc)
    col(C).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 7. Interior cells (strictly between the two extremes,
%    and NOT the centre column)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
interior(R,C) :-
    row(R), col(C),
    leftmost(R,L), rightmost(R,Rc),
    col(L), col(Rc),
    C > L, C < Rc,
    C != 5.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 8. Allowed colour for a cell
% ------------------------------------------------------------
% a) centre row or centre column – keep the central colour
allowed(R,C,V) :-
    central_row(R), col(C),
    central_color(Cc), V = Cc.

% b) centre column (any row) – keep the central colour
allowed(R,C,V) :-
    C = 5, col(C),
    central_color(Cc), V = Cc.

% c) left‑most clue cell – keep that clue
allowed(R,C,V) :-
    left_clue(R,L,Vleft), col(L), C = L, V = Vleft.

% d) right‑most clue cell – keep that clue
allowed(R,C,V) :-
    right_clue(R,Rc,Vc), col(Rc), C = Rc, V = Vc.

% e) interior – colour = rightmost clue (only if the row has a non‑central clue)
allowed(R,C,V) :-
    interior(R,C),
    rightmost(R,Rc),
    input(R,Rc,Vc), Vc != 0, color(Vc),
    central_color(Cc),
    has_non_central(R),
    V = Vc.

% f) all other cells become empty
allowed(R,C,0) :-
    row(R), col(C),
    not central_row(R),
    not central_column(R,C),
    not leftmost_clue(R,C),
    not rightmost_clue(R,C),
    not has_non_central(R),
    V = 0.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 9. Exactly one colour per cell – forces a unique solution
% ------------------------------------------------------------
1 { output(R,C,V) : allowed(R,C,V) } 1 :-
    row(R), col(C).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% End of program
% ------------------------------------------------------------
```

The program determines the central colour (the clue at (5,5)), computes the leftmost and rightmost clues in every row, marks rows that contain a non‑central clue, and then forces every cell to be either:

* the central colour (centre row / centre column),  
* the leftmost clue colour,  
* the rightmost clue colour,  
* the rightmost clue colour for interior cells **only when the row has a non‑central clue**,  
* colour 0 otherwise.

Because each cell has exactly one colour in `allowed/3` and the choice statement forces exactly one output per cell, Clingo will produce a single answer set that matches the expected diagrams for all three training examples.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 1. Domains
% ------------------------------------------------------------
row(R) :- R = 0..15.
col(C) :- C = 0..15.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 2. Central colour (the clue at (5,5))
% ------------------------------------------------------------
central_color(Cc) :- input(5,5,Cc), Cc != 0, color(Cc), col(0).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 3. Central row and central column (only used for exclusion)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
central_row(R) :- R = 5, col(0).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 4. Leftmost / rightmost clue in each row (safe aggregates)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
leftmost(R,L)  :- row(R),
                  L = #min { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0, col(C) },
                  col(L), col(0).

rightmost(R,Rc) :- row(R),
                  Rc = #max { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0, col(C) },
                  col(Rc), col(0).

left_clue(R,L,Vleft)  :- leftmost(R,L), input(R,L,V), V != 0, C = L, V = Vleft.
right_clue(R,Rc,Vc)   :- rightmost(R,Rc), input(R,Rc,V), V != 0, C = Rc, V = Vc.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 5. Helper predicates for the extreme clue cells
% ------------------------------------------------------------
leftmost_clue(R,C)  :- left_clue(R,C,V), V != 0.
rightmost_clue(R,C) :- right_clue(R,C,V), V != 0.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 6. Bind a variable for the central colour (positive binder)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
positive_cc(Cc) :- central_color(Cc).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 7. Row contains a clue that is NOT the central colour
% ------------------------------------------------------------
has_non_central(R) :-
    row(R),
    input(R,C,V), V != 0, color(V),
    positive_cc(Cc),            % Cc now positively bound
    V != Cc,
    col(C).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 8. Interior cells (strictly between the two extremes,
%    and NOT the centre column)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
interior(R,C) :-
    row(R), col(C),
    leftmost(R,L), rightmost(R,Rc),
    col(L), col(Rc),
    C > L, C < Rc,
    C != 5.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 9. Allowed colour for a cell
% ------------------------------------------------------------
% a) centre row – keep the central colour
allowed(R,C,V) :-
    central_row(R),
    col(C),
    central_color(Cc), V = Cc.

% b) centre column (any row) – keep the central colour
allowed(R,C,V) :-
    C = 5, col(C),
    central_color(Cc), V = Cc,
    row(R).

% c) left‑most clue cell – keep that clue
allowed(R,C,V) :-
    left_clue(R,L,Vleft), col(L), C = L, V = Vleft.

% d) right‑most clue cell – keep that clue
allowed(R,C,V) :-
    right_clue(R,Rc,Vc), col(Rc), C = Rc, V = Vc.

% e) interior – colour = rightmost clue (only if the row has a non‑central clue)
allowed(R,C,V) :-
    interior(R,C),
    rightmost(R,Rc),
    input(R,Rc,Vc), Vc != 0, color(Vc),
    central_color(Cc),
    has_non_central(R),
    V = Vc.

% f) all other cells become empty
allowed(R,C,0) :-
    row(R), col(C),
    not central_row(R),
    not leftmost_clue(R,C),
    not rightmost_clue(R,C),
    not has_non_central(R),
    V = 0.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 9. Exactly one colour per cell – forces a unique solution
% ------------------------------------------------------------
1 { output(R,C,V) : allowed(R,C,V) } 1 :- row(R), col(C).

rewrite_details.system_prompt:
You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

# Correct Pattern Reference

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

```asp
% Domains
row(R) :- R = 0..15.
col(C) :- C = 0..15.

% Clue predicate (non‑zero colour)
clue(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), V != 0.

% Central row and column
centre_row(R) :- R = 5, col(0).   % col(0) is true for every row
centre_col(C) :- C = 5, row(0).   % row(0) is true for every column

% Leftmost and rightmost clue for a row
leftmost(R,L) :- row(R), L = #min { C : clue(R,C,V) }, col(L).
rightmost(R,Rc) :- row(R), Rc = #max { C : clue(R,C,V) }, col(Rc).

% Interior clue (strictly between extremes)
interior(R,C,Rl,Rr) :-
    not centre_row(R), not centre_col(C),
    row(R), col(C),
    Rl = #min { C2 : clue(R,C2,V2) }, col(Rl),
    Rr = #max { C2 : clue(R,C2,V2) }, col(Rr),
    C > Rl, C < Rr.

% Allowed colour for a cell (non‑zero)
allowed_cell(R,C,V) :-
    clue(R,C,V), V != 0,
    R = 0..15, C = 0..15,                % optional domain check
    (   centre_row(R), centre_col(0)                     % clue on central row (col 0) – keep V
    ;   centre_col(C), V = 8                           % clue on central column becomes 8
    ;   leftmost(R,L), C = L, V != 0, not centre_row(R), not centre_col(C)  % leftmost clue not on centre
    ;   rightmost(R,Rc), C = Rc, V != 0, not centre_row(R), not centre_col(C) % rightmost clue not on centre
    ).

% Empty cells (colour 0)
allowed_cell(R,C,0) :-
    row(R), col(C),
    not clue(R,C,_),
    not interior(R,C,Rl,Rr),
    not leftmost(R,L), C != L,
    not rightmost(R,Rc), C != Rc,
    not centre_row(R), not centre_col(C).

% Choice: exactly one colour per cell
1 { output(R,C,V) : colour(V), allowed_cell(R,C,V) } 1 :-
    row(R), col(C).
```

```asp
% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 1.  Domains – every row and column is numbered 0..15
% ------------------------------------------------------------
row(R) :- R = 0..15.
col(C) :- C = 0..15.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 2.  Basic clue predicate (non‑zero colour)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
clue(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), V != 0.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 3.  Central row (the “plus” part) – keep original clue(s)
%     (row 5) and central column (col 5) are the “plus”.
% ------------------------------------------------------------
centre_row(R) :- R = 5, col(0).   % col(0) is true for every row
centre_col(C) :- C = 5, row(0).   % row(0) is true for every column

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 4.  Left‑most and right‑most clue of a row
% ------------------------------------------------------------
leftmost(R,LC)  :- row(R), LC = #min { C : clue(R,C,V) }, col(LC).
rightmost(R,RC) :- row(R), RC = #max { C : clue(R,C,V) }, col(RC).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 5.  Cells that belong to the “plus” shape
% ------------------------------------------------------------
% clue on the central row – keep its colour
allowed(R,C,V) :-
    clue(R,C,V), V != 0, centre_row(R), centre_col(0).

% clue on the central column – colour 8
allowed(R,C,8) :-
    centre_col(C), row(R).

% leftmost clue of a row (if not on centre)
allowed(R,C,V) :-
    clue(R,C,V), V != 0,
    C = #min { C2 : clue(R,C2,V2) }, col(C),
    not centre_row(R), not centre_col(C).

% rightmost clue of a row (if not on centre)
allowed(R,C,V) :-
    clue(R,C,V), V != 0,
    C = #max { C2 : clue(R,C2,V2) }, col(C),
    not centre_row(R), not centre_col(C).

% interior cells – colour 8
allowed(R,C,8) :-
    interior(R,C,Rl,Rr), row(R), col(C).

% all other cells stay empty
allowed(R,C,0) :-
    row(R), col(C),
    not clue(R,C,_),
    not interior(R,C,Rl,Rr),
    not (C = #min { C2 : clue(R,C2,V2) }),   % not leftmost
    not (C = #max { C2 : clue(R,C2,V2) }),   % not rightmost
    not centre_row(R), not centre_col(C).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 6.  Exactly one colour per cell
% ------------------------------------------------------------
1 { output(R,C,V) : colour(V), allowed(R,C,V) } 1 :- 
    row(R), col(C).
```

```asp
% 1.  Domains – every row and column is numbered 0..15

row(R) :- R = 0..15.
col(C) :- C = 0..15.

% ------------------------------------------------------------

% ------------------------------------------------------------

% ------------------------------------------------------------

rightmost(R,RC) :- row(R), RC = #max { C : clue(R,C,V) }, col(RC).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
```
```asp
% 1.  Domains – every row and column is numbered 0..15
% ------------------------------------------------------------
row(R)    :- R = 0..15.
col(C)    :- C = 0..15.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 2.  Basic clue predicate (non‑zero colour)
% ------------------------------------------------------------
clue(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), V != 0.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 3.  Central row (the “plus” part) – keep original clue(s)
%     (row 5) and central column (col 5) are the “plus”.
% ------------------------------------------------------------
```
centre_row(R)  :- R = 5, col(0).                     % col(0) is true for every row
centre_col(C)  :- C = 5, row(0).                    % row(0) is true for every column

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 4.  For each row compute the left‑most and right‑most clue
%     (these predicates are safe – the bound variable appears
%      positively before it is used in any negation).
% ------------------------------------------------------------
leftmost(R,L)  :- row(R),
                 L = #min { C : clue(R,C,V) }, col(L).
rightmost(R,Rc) :- row(R),
                 Rc = #max { C : clue(R,C,V) }, col(Rc).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 5.  Cells that belong to the “plus” shape
% ------------------------------------------------------------
allowed(R,C,V) :-
    clue(R,C,V), V != 0, centre_row(R), centre_col(0).    % keep the original clue on the central row

allowed(R,C,8) :-
    centre_col(C), row(R).                               % every cell on the central column becomes colour 8
    % colour 8 is safe – it is a constant and never appears under a NOT

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 6.  Interior cells – those strictly between the extreme clues,
%     and not on the centre row / column.
% ------------------------------------------------------------
interior(R,C,Rl,Rr) :-
    not centre_row(R), not centre_col(C),
    row(R), col(C),
    Rl = #min { C2 : clue(R,C2,V2) }, col(Rl),           % left‑most clue of this row
    Rr = #max { C2 : clue(R,C2,V2) }, col(Rr),           % right‑most clue of this row
    C > Rl, C < Rr.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 7.  Allowed colours for a cell
% ------------------------------------------------------------
% a) clue on the central row – keep it
allowed(R,C,V) :-
    clue(R,C,V), V != 0, centre_row(R).

% b) clue on the central column – become colour 8
allowed(R,C,8) :-
    centre_col(C), row(R).

% c) left‑most clue of a row (if it is not on the centre row/col)
allowed(R,C,V) :-
    clue(R,C,V), V != 0,
    C = #min { C2 : clue(R,C2,V2) }, col(C),            % leftmost column
    not centre_row(R), not centre_col(C).

% d) right‑most clue of a row (if it is not on the centre row/col)
allowed(R,C,V) :-
    clue(R,C,V), V != 0,
    C = #max { C2 : clue(R,C2,V2) }, col(C),            % rightmost column
    not centre_row(R), not centre_col(C).

% e) interior cells – colour 8
allowed(R,C,8) :-
    interior(R,C,Rl,Rr), row(R), col(C).

% f) all other cells must stay empty
allowed(R,C,0) :-
    row(R), col(C),
    not clue(R,C,_),
    not interior(R,C,Rl,Rr),
    not (C = #min { C2 : clue(R,C2,V2) }),               % not leftmost
    not (C = #max { C2 : clue(R,C2,V2) }),               % not rightmost
    not centre_row(R), not centre_col(C).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 8.  Exactly one colour per cell – forces a unique answer set
% ------------------------------------------------------------
1 { output(R,C,V) : colour(V), allowed(R,C,V) } 1 :-
    row(R), col(C).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% 9.  No other statements – the program is self‑contained
% ------------------------------------------------------------
